D4 Colorimetry L2 Understand Atom

Two Spectra, One Colour

tune three narrow bands until their colour matches a smooth spectrum — proof that wildly different light can look identical

Because the eye collapses every spectrum to three cone signals, completely different spectral power distributions can produce the very same colour. Such a pair is called a metameric match — the heart of why displays and prints work at all.

The smooth curve is fixed. Adjust the three narrow bands until your swatch matches it — ΔE under 2 is a metameric match. The spectra look nothing alike, yet the colour is the same.
